title-group.article-title: Post-Translational Regulation of Polycystin-2 Protein Expression as a Novel Mechanism of Cholangiocyte Reaction and Repair from Biliary Damage

article-meta.aff: 1Section of Digestive Diseases, Yale University, New Haven, Connecticut, USA
2Department of Molecular Medicine, University of Padova, Italy
3Department of Surgery and Interdisciplinary Medicine, University of Milan-Bicocca, Milan, Italy

author-notes.corresp: Correspondence: Mario Strazzabosco M.D., Ph.D, Dept. of Internal Medicine, Section of Digestive Diseases, Yale University School of Medicine, 333 Cedar Street LMP 1080, 06520 New Haven, CT USA, Phone: +1-203-785-7281, Fax: +1-203-785-7273, mario.strazzabosco@yale.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

related-article.article-title: Retracted: Posttranslational regulation of polycystin-2 protein expression as a novel mechanism of cholangiocyte reaction and repair from biliary damage

abstract.p: Polycystin-2 (PC2 /TRPP2), a member of the transient receptor potential channels (TRP) family, is a non-selective calcium channel. Mutations in PC2/TRPP2 are associated with Polycystic Liver Diseases. PC2-defective cholangiocytes shows increased production of cAMP, PKA-dependent activation of the ERK1/2 pathway, HIF1α-mediated VEGF production, and stimulation of cyst growth and progression. Activation of the ERK/HIF1α/VEGF pathway in cholangiocytes plays a key role during repair from biliary damage. We hypothesized that PC2 levels are modulated during biliary damage/repair, resulting in activation of the ERK/HIF1α/VEGF pathway.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: PC2 protein expression, but not its gene expression, was significantly reduced in mouse livers with biliary damage (Mdr2−/−-KO, bile duct ligation, DDC-treatment). Treatment of colangiocytes with pro-inflammatory cytokines, nitric oxide (NO) donors and ER stressors), increased ERK1/2 phosphorylation, HIF1α transcriptional activity, secretion of VEGF, VEGFR2 phosphorylation and downregulated PC2 protein expression without affecting PC2 gene expression. Expression of Herp and NEK, ubiquitin-like proteins that promote proteosomal PC2 degradation was increased. Pre-treatment with the proteasome inhibitor MG-132 restored the expression of PC2 in cells treated with cytokines but not in cells treated with NO donors or with ER stressors. In these conditions, PC2 degradation was instead inhibited by interfering with the autophagy pathway. Treatment of DDC-mice and of Mdr2−/−-mice with the proteasome inhibitor bortezomib, restored PC2 expression and significantly reduced the ductular reaction, fibrosis and p-ERK1/2. In conclusion, in response to biliary damage, PC2 expression is modulated post-translationally by the proteasome or the autophagy pathways. PC2-dowregulation is associated with activation of ERK1/2 and increase of HIF1α-mediated VEGF secretion. Treatments able to restore PC2 expression and to reduce ductular reaction and fibrosis may represent a new therapeutic approach in biliary diseases.

body.p: Polycystin 2 (PC2 or TRPP2) is one of the proteins (polycystin 1 and 2) defective in Autosomal Polycystic Kidney Disease, a disease characterized by the formation of multiple cysts in the liver, kidney and pancreas (1). PC2 is localized in the cilium and at the plasma membrane, but the largest pool of PC2 is actually located in the ER membrane (2, 3). PC2 is member of the transient receptor potential channels (TRP) family, functions as a non-selective Ca2+channel, as a mechano-chemo-osmo-sensor, or a receptor operated Ca2+ channel, depending on its interaction with PC1, TRPV4, or ryanodine receptors (4–6). In the ER, PC2 interacts with ryanodine and Insp3 receptors and participates to the regulation of agonist-induced Ca2+ release from the ER (5, 6). Nevertheless, the exact function and physiologic roles of PC2 remain largely unknown.

In PC2/TRPP2-defective cholangiocytes, store-operated calcium entry (SOCE) is inhibited, and cells respond to an acute changes in ER Ca2+ concentration by stimulating cAMP production through the translocation of ER Ca2+ sensors (STIM1) and the activation of membrane adenylyl cyclases (7). We have also shown that in in PC2-defective cholangiocytes, altered cellular Ca2+ homeostasis leads to increased levels of cAMP and to PKA/Ras/Raf/ERK1/2-mediated stimulation of HIF-1α and VEGF production (8–10). Using mice with conditional PC2 defect we have shown that up-regulation of the above pathway promotes cyst growth through the autocrine stimulation of cystic epithelium proliferation and a paracrine effect on pericystic vasculature (10). In fact, inhibition of VEGFR2 receptors in PC-2 defective mice significantly reduces disease progression (10). The hypothesis of the current study is that PC2 levels in cholangiocytes can be modulated during biliary damage and repair, thereby regulating ERK/HIF1a activity, VEGF secretion and biliary repair.

Recent studies have shown that, rather than being restricted to the vascular endothelium, VEGF, together with its receptors, is expressed and functional also in some epithelial cells during development and in diseased conditions (11). In cholangiocytes, VEGF-A appears to regulate cell proliferation and cross-talk and represents a mechanism that drives the reparative response to damage in chronic cholangiopathies (11, 12).

Our study show that PC2 expression is not constitutive, but can be modulated in pathophysiological conditions. In particular, PC2 protein expression is specifically reduced in mice with biliary damage, being modulated post-translationally by pro-inflammatory cytokines, through the ERAD/proteasome pathway (ER-associated degradation) and by ER-stressors and NO-donors through the autophagy pathway. Downregulation of PC2 protein expression in cholangiocytes increases ERK1/2/HIF1α signaling and VEGF secretion. Treatment with proteasome inhibitor bortezomib was able to restore PC2 expression in DCC treated mice and in Mdr2−/− mice. These novel findings show that modulation of PC2 levels play a key role in the regulation of cholangiocyte response to biliary damage and suggest potential therapeutic targets.

sec.title: Materials and reagents
sec.p: All reagents were obtained from Sigma Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO), unless otherwise indicated. Culture media, Dulbecco/Vogt modified Eagle's minimal essential medium (DMEM), HAM’s F12, fetal bovine serum, MEM non-essential amino acids solution, MEM vitamin solutions, glyceryl monostearate, chemically defined lipid concentrate, soybean trypsin inhibitor, penicillin/streptomycin, gentamycin and glutamine were purchased from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA).

sec.title: Mouse models of cholestasis
sec.p: C57BL/6J mice (8–9 weeks old) were obtained from the Jackson Laboratory, Bar Harbor, ME. Mice were housed in the Yale animal facility, and were kept in controlled light 12-h light, 12-h dark cycle) and temperature (22 °C), and provided with food and water ad libitum. All animal experimental protocols were approved by the Yale Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC). Different models of liver injury were used: a) Bile duct ligation. BDL was performed as previously described (13) and mice were sacrificed after two weeks; b) DDC: mice were exposed to 3,5-diethoxycarbonyl-1,4-dihydrocollidine (DDC) to induce biliary damage (14). DDC treatment, used to mimic intrahepatic obstructive cholestasis, was added to the diet at a concentration of 0.1% (BioServ, Inc. Frenchtown, NJ) for 10 days (14); c) APAP was administered to over-night fasted mice at the dose of 500mg/Kg I.P and dissolved in saline buffer (15). Mdr2−/− mice (a kind gift from Dr J.L. Boyer, Yale University). DDC-treated and Mdr2−/− mice were treated with bortezomib 1 mg/Kg, administered intravenously twice a week.

sec.title: Cell isolation and treatments
sec.p: Mouse cholangiocytes were isolated as previously described (8–10). Cells were treated for 24 hrs with IL-1β (20 U/ml), INFγ ((100 U/ml), TNFα (500 U/ml), nitric oxide (NO) donors (DETAnonoate, 250 µM), ER stressors (Thapsigargin, 2 µM). In presence or not of proteasome inhibitors (MG132, 100 nM), PI3K inhibitors (LY294002, 5 µM; Wortmannin, 10 nM) or lysosomal inhibitors (Chloroquine, 10 µM).

sec.title: Immunoprecipitation and Western blot
sec.p: Tipically 20 and 1000 µg of total protein were used for immunoblotting and immunoprecipitation, respectively. Nuclear and cytosolic fractions were isolated using the NE-PER Kit (Pierce, Rockford, IL), according to the manufacturer's instructions. Total cell lysates were extracted using a lysate buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, 1% NP40, 0.1% SDS, 0.1% Deoxycholic acid, 0.1 mM EDTA, 0.1 mM EGTA) containing fresh protease and phosphatase inhibitor cocktails (Sigma, St Louis, CA). Protein concentration was measured using the Comassie protein assay reagent (Pierce, Rockford, IL). To immune-precipitate PC2 1 mg of proteins were incubated with the proper primary antibody for 1 h at 4°C under rotation (see below). Samples were then incubated with 30 µl of Protein A/G PLUS-Agarose for 3 h at 4°C under rotation. After several washing, the beads were resuspended in 50 µl of 4× NuPAGE Lithium Dodecyl Sulphate (LDS) sample buffer. Western blot with an antibody against ubiquitin was performed on the immune-precipitated lysates. For Western blots, equal amounts of total lysate were applied to a 4–12% NuPAGE® Novex Bis-Tris gel (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA) and electrophoresed. Proteins were transferred to nitrocellulose membrane (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA). Membranes were blocked with 5% non-fat dry milk (Bio-Rad Laboratories) in phosphate-buffered saline containing 0.1% Tween-20 (PBST) for 1h and then incubated with specific primary antibodies overnight. Nitrocellulose membranes were washed three times with PBST and then incubated with horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibodies for 1h. Proteins were visualized by enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL Plus kit; Amersham Biosciences, Piscataway, NJ, USA). The intensity of the bands was determined by scanning video densitometry using the Total lab Tl120DM software (Non linear USA Inc, Durham NC). The following antibodies were used: PC2 (a kind gift form Dr Somlo, Yale University), PC2, VEGF, NEK-1, HERP (Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Dallas TX) and LC3-II (Cell Signaling Technology, Danvers MA).

sec.title: Measurement of VEGF Secretion in Cultured Cells
sec.p: An enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (R&D Systems, Minneapolis MN) was used to quantify VEGF in culture medium collected from cholangiocytes isolated from polycystic and control mice, as we previously described (8–10). Briefly, medium was incubated with a highly purified antibody coated onto 96-well plates. A VEGF standard curve was generated for each individual experiment. Readings were normalized for the total protein in the well.

sec.title: Determination of HIF-1α transcriptional activity in Nuclear Fractions in Cultured Cells
sec.p: Cells were incubated in the presence of IL-1β (20 U/ml), INFγ ((100 U/ml), TNFα (500 U/ml), (DETAnonoate, 250 µM) or (Thapsigargin, 2 µM) for 24hrs and HIF1α transcriptional activity has been detected in nuclear fraction using a HIF1α transcription activity assay kit (Cayman Chemicals) following the protocol from the manufacturer and normalized to the amount of nuclear protein.

sec.title: Gene Expression Assessment by Real-Time PCR
sec.p: Total RNA was isolated from cholangiocytes using TRIzol Reagent (Invitrogen) according to the manufacturer's instructions. Briefly, 800 ng RNA were converted into a PCR template using the TaqMan Reverse Transcription Kit (Applied Biosystems), which was then used for the real-time PCR analysis using commercially available specific FAM conjugated probes for PC2 and GAPDH (Applied Biosystems) in combination with the Fast Start Universal Probe Master mix (Rox) (Roche Diagnostics, Indianapolis, IN) on an Applied Biosystems 7500 Real-Time PCR system. Data were normalized against the housekeeping gene and analyzed using the ΔΔCt method.

sec.title: Immunohistochemical analysis
sec.p: Immunohistochemistry was performed on paraffin sections to quantify bile duct proliferation (cytokeratin 19, K19) and to quantify the amount of pERK1/2 (double staining K19/pERK1/2). Antigen retrieval using 1 mM EDTA, pH 8.0, in a steamer was done prior to overnight incubation with the monoclonal rat anti-CK19 (Troma III, DSHB 1:100) or with the polyclonal anti rabbit pERK1/2 (Cell Signaling, Technology, Danvers MA 1:50) as previously described (10, 14). Quantification of K19 positive cells was performed using a computer-assisted morphometric analysis (MetaMorph software, Ontario, NY) using a motorized stage system able to scan, at 4× magnification, the whole liver lobes. Data were expressed as the percentage of the whole liver lobe area occupied by K19 cells. The setup consisted of a Nikon Eclipse TE2000U microscope (Nikon, Avon, MA), a motorized stage system (Roper Scientific, Trenton, NJ), and a Photometrics Cool Snap HQ digital camera (Photometrics, Tucson, AZ). To quantify p-ERK1/2 positive we measured the percentage of pERK1/2-K19 positive cells with respect to K19 positive cells in ten fields taken at the magnification of 200× per liver lobe, using a photometric cool snap HQ digital camera (Roper Scientific).

sec.title: Assessment of portal fibrosis by Sirius Red staining
sec.p: Sirius red histochemistry was performed in liver sections obtained from Mdr2−/− and WT mice. Sections stained by Sirius Red were analyzed with a Nikon Eclipse TE2000U microscope at polarized light (Nikon, USA) by calculating the positive area by the ImageJ software (28) at 200× magnification in 10 random micrographs taken from each main liver lobe for each animal. Portal fibrosis was calculated as percentage of pixels above the threshold value with respect to the total pixels per portal area.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Results are shown as mean±standard deviation. Statistical comparisons were made using Student’s t tests, or one-way ANOVA, where appropriate. The statistical analysis was performed using SAS software (SAS, Cary, NC); p values <0.05 were considered as significant.

sec.title: PC2 protein but not gene expression is modulated in mouse models of cholestatic liver diseases
sec.p: PC2 expression was assessed in liver tissues obtained from mice in which a biliary damage was induced by the ligation of the bile duct (BDL), a well-known model of extra-hepatic cholestasis, or by treatment with dehydrocollidine (DDC), a compound that causes an intrahepatic obstructive cholestasis, or APAP a model of hepatocellular damage. As shown in Figure 1A, PC2 expression, assessed by Western Blot for PC2 on whole liver tissue lysate, was significantly reduced with respect to untreated mice in biliary damages, such as BDL (66%, p<0.001, n=3), DDC-treated mice (59%, p<0.001, n=3) and Mdr2−/− mice (53% vs WT, p<0.001, n=3) (Figure 1A,B). On the other hand PC2 protein expression was unchanged in APAP-induced acute hepatitis. These data suggest that PC2 expression is modulated in biliary disease of different nature (obstructive, genetic, toxic), but not in acute hepatocellular damage, consistent with the cholangiocyte-restricted PC2 expression in the liver. Furthermore, the decrease in PC2 protein content was due to increased PC2 degradation, rather than decreased gene expression. In fact, PC2 gene expression assessed by RT-PCR on the same samples, was not reduced (Figure 1C).

sec.title: PC2 protein expression is modulated in cholangiocytes exposed to pro-inflammatory cytokines, NO-donors or ER-stressors
sec.p: To understand the cellular mechanisms involved in PC2 modulation we challenged cultured mouse cholangiocytes with factors known to be involved in biliary damage. Cholangiocytes were thus exposed to pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β (20 U/ml), IFNγ (100 U/ml), TNFα (500 U/ml)) (16) given as a single agent or in combination, or to nitric oxide donors (DETAnonoate, 250 µM) (17) or ER stressors (thapsigargin, 2 µM) (7). Results, shown in figure 2A, indicate that all the above treatments significantly reduced the expression of the PC2 protein. Similarly to the results obtained in cholestatic mice, the gene expression of PC2 remained unchanged (Figure 2B). Noteworthy, in the same conditions, HIF1α transcription activity (Figure 2C), and secretion of VEGF were both significantly increased (Figure 2D). Furthermore, similarly to what previously shown in PC2 defective cholangiocytes, the increased secretion of VEGF was mediated by an increased phosphorylation of ERK1/2 (Figure 3A) and of VEGF receptor 2 (VEGFR2) (Figure 3B).

sec.title: The ERAD pathway is responsible for PC2 degradation in cholangiocytes exposed to pro-inflammatory cytokines
sec.p: Previous studies in kidney cells have shown that PC2 is a substrate for Endoplasmic Reticulum Associate Degradation (ERAD) through the 26S ubiquitin-proteasome system (18). To confirm that PC2 degradation is proteasome-dependent also in cholangiocytes we studied the effects of proteasome inhibitors (MG132) on cycloheximide-pretreated cells. As shown in Figure 4A, MG132 fully prevented the cycloheximide-induced decrease in PC2 protein levels. As shown in figure 4B, ubiquitination status of PC2 in cholangiocytes was significantly increased in cells treated with pro-inflammatory cytokines with respect to the untreated controls. Furthermore, we found that treatment with cytokines significantly increased the expression of Nek1 and Herp (Figure 4C,D), two ubiquitin-like proteins, involved in the regulation of ERAD (18, 19) (18, 20) Nek1 and Herp have been shown to promote PC2 degradation by increasing the retrotranslocation of PC2 from the ER to the proteasome complex in the cytosol (18). Finally, the proteasome inhibitor MG132 prevented the degradation of PC2 induced by cytokines (Figure 5), confirming that this is mediated by proteasome. However, treatment with MG132 did not suppressed the degradation of PC2 induced by Detanonoate and thapsigargin (Figure 5A,B), suggesting that in this case, PC2 in degradated by a different mechanisms.

sec.title: PC2 modulation during ER stress is mediated by autophagy
sec.p: To better understand the mechanisms responsible for PC2 degradation in cells treated with NO donors and ER stressors, we studied the role of autophagy, a major pathway involved in protein degradation (20). LC3-II, a microtubule-associate protein light chain is a well-known marker of autophagy, specifically of increased formation of autophagosomes (21). As shown in figure 6C, the levels of LC3II increased after treatment with thapsigargin and DETAnonoate, suggesting activation of authophagy. As a further indication of the role of autophagy, we inhibited the formation the autophagosome, using inhibitors of PI3K (LY294002 and wortmannin), or blocked the formation of the phagolysosome with chloroquine. As shown in figure 6 A,B, both LY294002 and wortmannin restored the levels of PC2 expression in cholangiocytes treated with NO donors or with ER stressors. Furthermore, LY294002 and wortmannin significantly decreased the expression of LC3-II induced by thapsigargin and DETAnonoate (Figure 6 C,D).

sec.title: Proteosome inhibition restores PC2 expression in mice treated with DCC and in MDR2−/− mice and reduce ductular reaction and pERK1/2
sec.p: To determine the pathophysiological relevance of our findings, we treated mice in vivo with the proteasome inhibitor bortezomib (1 mg/Kg, administered intravenously twice a week). In mice treated with DDC, bortezomib, significantly reduced the extent of ductular reaction, quantified as K19 positive staining, and restored the protein expression of PC2 (Figure 7A,B). Also in Mdr2−/− mice (treated for three weeks from P28 to P49) Bortezomib significantly reduced the K19 positive area and restored the expression of PC2. Furthermore, Bortezomib significantly reduced liver fibrosis, assessed by Sirius red staining, in Mdr2−/− mice (Figure 8) and the number of pERK1/2 positive cells both in DDC treated mice (Figure 7A) and in Mdr2−/− mice (Figure 8A). Finally, Bortezomib (20 nM) reduced the secretion of VEGF in cholangiocytes treated with pro-inflammatory cytokines (Suppl. Figure 1).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our results show that PC2 expression in not constitutive, but PC2 levels can be regulated by a number of factors involved in biliary damage and acting through two main pathways, ERAD for pro-inflammatory cytokines and autophagy for ER stressors and NO donors. These novel findings shed further light on the mechanisms of biliary repair by describing a new mechanism characterized by the post-translational regulation of PC2 and activation of ERK/1/2, HIF-1α and VEGF, a fundamental pathway involved in the regulation of ductular reaction.

The main findings of this study are: 1) the expression of PC2 protein is downregulated in mice with biliary damage; 2) post-translational downregulation of PC2 expression can be induced by pro-inflammatory cytokines, ER-stressors and NO-donors 3) PC2 degradation is differentially mediated by ERAD/proteasome pathway during inflammation and by autophagy during ER-stress; 4) reduced PC2 protein expression in cholangiocytes in accompanied by increases ERK1/2 phosphorylation, HIF1α transcriptional activity, VEGF secretion and VEGFR2 phosphorylation; 5) in two different models of biliary damage, in vivo treatment with proteasome inhibitors reduces ductular reaction, liver fibrosis, pERK1/2 and restores PC2 expression.

To repair the damage to the biliary epithelium, cholangiocytes must proliferate and then form branching tubular structures (22, 23). These “reactive” cholangiocytes are characterized by motile properties and by the active production of growth factors and cytokines, by which they establish an extensive cross-talk with multiple cell types (22–24). While this response is necessary to repair the biliary damage, the undesired effect of these mechanisms is the generation of an exaggerated neo-angiogenic response with production of new fibro-vascular stroma and progressive liver fibrosis (11, 25). The intense remodelling activity characteristic of biliary repair is closely associated with adaptive vascular responses (11, 26). This is for example necessary to support the vascularization of the cystic epithelium in congenital ciliary protein defects (8–10, 27), and the generation of the tumour reactive stroma in cholangiocarcinoma (28–30).

These mechanisms also maintain an adequate blood supply during liver repair, favour the proliferation of HPC/reactive cholangiocytes and promote a correct morphogenesis of branching tubular structures (11, 25, 26). However, in the context of non-resolving inflammation, reactive cholangiocyte express inflammatory and chemotactic cytokines leading to the recruitment of inflammatory and mesenchymal cells, thereby potentiating fibrosis (22, 23, 31). The mechanisms that induce “reactive” cholangiocytes to re-express growth factors, and morphogens enabling an active cross-talk between biliary, mesenchymal, vascular and inflammatory cells are still largely unknown. The study of genetically-determined biliary diseases offer a unique opportunity to better understand these mechanisms.

We have previously shown that PC2-defective cholangiocytes are characterized by changes in intracellular calcium homeostasis, leading to inappropriate production of cAMP, increased PKA-dependent activation of the ERK1/2 pathway and HIF1α mediated stimulation of VEGF production (7–10). Since production of VEGF is one of the major responses of cholangiocyte to biliary damage we investigated the hypothesis that PC2 may be modulated in biliary diseases. Our data show that PC2 protein expression is indeed modulated in mouse models of chemical-induced biliary damage as well as in a genetic model (Mdr2−/−), but not in acute hepatocellular damage. On the contrary to protein expression, gene expression of PC2 is not affected, suggesting increased degradation of PC2. Furthermore, factors involved in biliary damage, including cytokines, nitric oxide donors and ER stressors significantly decreased PC2 protein expression, but not its gene expression. Along with the decrease in PC2 expression, ERK1/2 phosphorylation, HIF1α transcriptional activity VEGF secretion and VEGFR2 phosphorylation were increased. We did not notice presence of liver cysts similar to polycystic liver diseases in DDC-treated or Mdr2−/− mice. In our conditions in fact, PC2 down-regulation do not reach the threshold that is necessary to cause cyst formation (32). In fact, our results show a residual expression of PC2 either in vivo in the different models as well as in in vitro challenged cholangiocytes.

Little is known on the regulation of PC2 protein levels. Recent work has shown that PC2 is a substrate for endoplasmic reticulum associated degradation pathway (ERAD) and undergo or proteosomal degradation (18, 33) (18, 32) (18, 19). ERAD substrates are ubiquitinated by E3 ubiquitin ligases. Herp, an ubiquitin-like protein, is implicated in the regulation of ERAD and appears to promote PC2 degradation by increasing the retrotranslocation of PC2 from the ER to the proteasome complex in the cytosol (18). The complex PC2-Herp is then ubiquitinated by the TAZ-regulated SCFβ-Trcp E3 ligase complex (33). TAZ is an adaptor of the E3 ubiquitin ligase complex that, when phosphorylated at his S309 residue, is able to target PC2 for degradation; S309 phosphorylation of TAZ depends on Nek1, a serine/threonine kinase that can be activated by genotoxic and inflammatory stress (33–35) (19, 33, 34). Our data showing an increase in Herp and NEK expression are consistent with the activation of the ERAD pathway. Moreover, the involvement of proteasome degradation is confirmed by the ability of the proteasome inhibitor MG132 to block the decrease on PC2 expression induced by inflammatory cytokines.

Notably, proteasome inhibitors did not restore the expression of PC2 in cells treated with NO-donors or ER stressor. Our study provides circumstantial evidence that in these conditions autophagy rather than ERAD may be involved. In fact, inhibition of the formation of the autophagosome (PI3 kinase inhibitors) or blockade of the autophagolysosome assembly (chloroquine) were able to restore the levels of PC2 in these conditions. Further evidence of the role of autophagy in response to NO donors and ER stressors is the increase in microtubule-associated protein light chain 3, a component of the autophagosome membrane and a well-accepted marker of autophagy (20). These data clearly suggest that degradation of PC2 is finely and differentially regulated, depending the specific conditions associated with biliary damage and open a new avenue to understand the mechanisms responsible for cholangiocyte response to biliary damage.

In aggregate our data indicate that PC2 protein expression is modulated post-translationally by pro-inflammatory cytokines, ER-stressors and NO-donors in mice with biliary damage. Down regulation of PC2 protein expression in cholangiocytes increases the ERK1/2/HIF1α pathway, VEGF secretion and VEGFR2 activity, thereby playing a pivotal role in the regulation of cholangiocyte response to biliary damage. The finding that treatment with proteasome inhibitor restores the levels of PC2 and reduces biliary reaction, fibrosis, pERK1/2 and secretion of VEGF is of great interest and could represent a new therapeutic target in cholangiopathies.

ack.p: Financial Support: Supported by NIH Grant DK079005 to MS, by a NIH Grant DK34989: Silvio O. Conte Digestive Diseases Research Core Centers – 5P30DK034989NIH to MS and CS.

def.p: Homocysteine-Responsive Endoplasmic Reticulum-Resident Ubiquitin-Like Domain Member 1 Protein
Transient Receptor Potential Cation Channel, Subfamily V, Member 4
Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor Receptor Type 2

caption.title: PC2 protein but not gene expression is modulated in mouse models of cholestatic liver disease
caption.p: Liver tissues from Controls (Ctrl) untreated, bile duct ligated (BDL), DDC and APAP treated mice as well as from Mdr2−/− mice, were harvested and processed to isolate proteins or RNA. Western Blot (A) revealed a significant down-regulation of PC2 expression in livers from BDL, DDC treated mice and Mdr2−/− mice with respect to Ctrl and APAP-treated mice. The relative quantification is reported in the bar graphs (B). On the contrary, PC2 gene expression assessed by RT-PCR was not significantly down-regulated (C) (*p<0.001 vs C; n=4).

caption.title: Pro inflammatory cytokines, Nitric Oxide and ER stressors reduce PC2 expression and increase HIF-1α activity and VEGF secretion in cholangiocytes
caption.p: Cholangiocytes isolated form WT mice were treated with (IL-1β (20 U/ml), IFNγ (100 U/ml), TNFα (500 U/ml) or a mix of the three cytokines (MIX)), nitric oxide donors (DETAnonoate, 250 µM) and ER stressors (thapsigargin, 2 µM) for 24 hours. Western Blot on whole cell lysates revealed a significant down-regulation of PC2 expression in treated with respect to Ctrl cholangiocytes(A), while gene expression remained unchanged (B). Furthermore, in the same experimental conditions, HIF1α transcriptional activity (C) as well as VEGF secretion (D) were significantly increased in treated with respect to controls cholangiocytes (*p<0.001 vs Ctrl; n=4).

caption.title: Pro inflammatory cytokines and ER stressors increase phosphorylation of ERK1/2 and of VEGFR2 in mouse cholangiocytes
caption.p: Cholangiocytes isolated form WT mice were treated with a mix of the three pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β (20 U/ml), IFNγ (100 U/ml), TNFα (500 U/ml) (Cyt), nitric oxide donors (DETAnonoate, 250 µM) and ER stressors (thapsigargin, 2 µM) for 24hours. Western blot analysis revealed that ERK1/2 (A) and VEGFR2 phosphorylation (B) were significantly increased. (*p<0.001 vs C; n=4).

caption.title: The ERAD pathway is responsible for PC2 degradation in cholangiocytes exposed to pro-inflammatory cytokines
caption.p: (A) Mouse cholangiocytes were treated for 24 hours with cycloheximide (50 µg/ml) (CHX) or with a combination of (IL-1β (20 U/ml), IFNγ (100 U/ml), TNFα (500 U/ml) (MIX). Western blot, performed on whole cell lysate revealed a significant reduction of PC2 expression. However, pre-treatment with the proteasome inhibitor MG132 (100 nM), completely blocked the cycloheximide- or cytokines-induced decrease in PC2 levels. (B) PC2 ubiquitination. Mouse cholangiocytes were treated with a combination of (IL-1β (20 U/ml), IFNγ (100 U/ml), TNFα (500 U/ml) for 24 hours followed by protein preparation and immunoprecipitation with PC2 antibody. Precipitated ubiquitin was detected by immunoblotting with the ubiquitin antibody. Ubiquitinated PC2 is clearly evident in cytokines treated cells. (Representative experiment of four replicas). Cholangiocytes isolated form WT mice were treated with (IL-1β (20 U/ml), IFNγ (100 U/ml), TNFα (500 U/ml) or a mix of them (MIX)), nitric oxide donors (DETAnonoate, 250 µM) and ER stressors (thapsigargin, 2 µM). NEK1 (C) or HERP (D) expression was assessed by Western blot. (*p<0.001 vs Ctrl; **p<0.01 Vs CHX; ◦p<0.01 vs MIX; ●p<0.01 vs MIX and vs MG; n=4)

caption.title: Proteasome inhibition (MG132) restores PC2 expression in inflammatory but not in ER stress conditions
caption.p: Pre-treatment with the proteasome inhibitor MG132 (100 nM) restored PC2 expression in mouse cholangiocytes treated with TNFα, IFNγ, IL-1β as a single agent or in combination (Mix), but not in cholangiocytes treated with DETAnonoate or Thapsigargin (*p<0.01 vs Ctrl; n=5).

caption.title: PC2 downregulation in mouse cholangiocytes treated with ER-stressors or NO donors is blocked by agents interfering with the autophagy pathway
caption.p: Inhibition of the formation of the autophagosome (LY294002, 5 µM; Wortmannin, 10 nM) and of the assembly of autophagolysosome (chloroquine 10µM), restored PC2 expression in mouse cholangiocytes treated with Thapsigargin or DETAnonoate (A) and (B). Furthermore, in cells treated with thapsigargin (2 µM) and DETAnonoate (250 µM), Western blot analysis revealed an increased expression of LC3-II a well-known marker of activated autophagy, that was significantly inhibited by wortmannin (C) as well as by LY294002 (D) (*p<0.01 vs Ctrl; n=5)

caption.title: Proteasome inhibition (Bortezomib) reduced the biliary damage and p-ERK1/2 in DDC treated mice
caption.p: Treatment with Bortezomib (1mg/Kg twice a week) in mice treated with DDC (0.1% diet, 10 days) significantly reduce the extent of ductular reaction as shown by the staining for the cholangiocyte-specific marker CK19 and the percentage of p-ERK1/2 positive cell (A). Moreover, treatment with Bortezomib restored the expression of PC2 expression assessed by Western blot in whole lysate form the same livers (B) (*p<0.001 vs Ctrl, *p<0.01 vs DDC) (Ctrl n=4; DDC n= 6; DDC+Bort n= 7).

caption.title: Proteasome inhibition (Bortezomib) reduced the biliary damage, liver fibrosis and p-ERK1/2 in Mdr2−/− mice
caption.p: Treatment with Bortezomib (1mg/Kg twice a week) in Mdr2−/− mice significantly reduce the extent of ductular reaction as shown by the staining for the cholangiocyte-specific marker CK19, reduced biliary fibrosis (Sirius red staining) and the percentage of pERK1/2 positive cells (A). Moreover, treatment with Bortezomib restored the expression of PC2 expression assessed by Western blot in whole liver lysate (B) (*p<0.001 vs Ctrl, *p<0.01 vs untreated Mdr2−/−) (Ctrl n=4; DDC n= 6; DDC+Bort n= 7).
